DAVOS, Switzerland, Jan 20 - The standoff with Iran over accounting for its stock of highly enriched uranium and inspecting nuclear facilities bombed by the United States and Israel cannot go on forever, U.N. nuclear watchdog chief Rafael Grossi said on Tuesday. The International Atomic Energy Agency has inspected all 13 declared nuclear facilities in Iran that were not bombed, but has been unable to inspect any of the three key sites that were bombed in June - Natanz, Fordow or Isfahan - Grossi told Reuters in an interview at the World Economic Forum in Davos. Iran must first file a report to the IAEA on what happened to those sites and material, including an estimated 440.9 kg of uranium enriched to up to 60% purity, close to the roughly 90% weapons-grade level. That is enough material, if enriched further, for 10 nuclear bombs, according to an IAEA yardstick. NO 'A LA CARTE' OPTION Iran has not submitted that special report to the IAEA. "This cannot go on forever because at some point, I will have to say, 'Well, I don't have any idea where this material is,'" which would mean there was no guarantee the material had not been diverted or hidden, Grossi said. "I do not have that conviction or conclusion at the moment, but what we are saying to Iran is that they need to engage." Iran says it is fully cooperating with the IAEA. Its government could not immediately be reached for comment. It is now at least seven months since the IAEA last verified Iran's stock of highly enriched uranium. Its own guidance is that it should be done monthly. Grossi said he was exercising "diplomatic prudence," but that Iran had to meet its obligations as a party to the nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty. - Portugal runoff, Bangladesh election-party reconsideration, World Cup boycott-petition association, Gaza child evacuation, Bisan Owda platform ban, Super Bowl markets, and Ukraine IAEA meeting had no direct naming signal strong enough to move.

## 2026-01-16 Update

Resolution events:
- United Democracy Project central New Jersey target resolved to Tom Malinowski. I missed by overfocusing on current progressive targets such as Sue Altman and Adeel Mangi. Lesson: AIPAC/UDP-style primary questions can target former members and comeback candidates, not only sitting-office progressives.
- Indonesian missing fisheries surveillance plane operator resolved to Indonesia Air Transport. I missed with better-known small operators. Lesson: include exact Indonesian charter/state-linked aviation companies when the question wording says officials will identify an operator.
- Karachi biggest fire site resolved to Gul Plaza. I missed. Lesson: major Karachi commercial centers such as Gul Plaza should be in the prior for fire-site markets.
- SDF assault target resolved to Raqqa. I missed after overweighting Deir Hafer/Maskana from visible front-line reporting. Lesson: for broad SDF/Syrian army assault questions, keep Raqqa high even when articles emphasize smaller Aleppo-front towns.

Updates made:
- Syria/SDF follow-on: Jan. 16 Al Jazeera and Reuters/Straits Times reporting said the Syrian army launched strikes in Deir Hafer, SDF would withdraw east of the Euphrates, and Damascus would deploy to vacated areas. The Raqqa resolution on the related assault-target question is a strong hidden signal, so I moved the complete-control city market toward Raqqa while retaining Deir Hafer/Maskana/Aleppo. Raised Syrian army modestly for eventual al-Shaddadi control.
- Ukraine/Russia/US talks: Zelensky said a delegation was heading to the US for security-guarantee talks in Miami, with possible signing at Davos next week, while earlier Kremlin reporting still kept Moscow alive for Witkoff/Kushner channels. Added Davos and Miami to the Jan. 23-24 host-city market and added Switzerland to the mediator-country market while keeping the US first.
- Gaza Board of Peace: the White House/Al Jazeera founding list named Tony Blair, Jared Kushner, Marco Rubio, Steve Witkoff, Marc Rowan, Ajay Banga and Robert Gabriel, with no Bangladesh or Pakistan government representative. Raised India as a hedge because Banga is the only clearly South Asian-linked founding figure, but kept Bangladesh/Pakistan live because the resolution criteria require country membership or government announcements rather than individual heritage.
- Bangladesh cricket: Hindustan Times/PTI reported the ICC will send officials to Dhaka, the BCB position on traveling to India remains unchanged, but the ICC is not expected to shift venues and will reassure Bangladesh on security. Kept Bangladesh very high for the Pakistan-support-by-boycott market, but reduced confidence in a replacement team and trimmed UAE/Oman/Nepal accordingly.
- Bangladesh election: Jan. 16 coverage emphasized Awami League exclusion and broader fairness concerns but did not identify the foreign-citizenship eligibility dispute. Added Awami League as a small tail and kept BNP/Jamaat/NCP as the main parties likely to threaten reconsideration.
- AFCON penalty: no direct penalty-taker signal before Morocco-Senegal, but final-preview coverage centered Morocco and Hakimi as a key/captain figure. Shifted a little probability from Brahim Diaz to Achraf Hakimi while keeping Brahim first.
- Iran diplomacy: Jan. 16 coverage said Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Oman and Egypt conducted intense diplomacy to stop a threatened US attack, and Russia/Putin offered mediation. Updated the later Iran-talks host/intermediary markets to include Saudi Arabia and Egypt more prominently, while keeping Oman/Qatar as the formal-talks leaders.

Watched but no update:
- Portugal runoff, southern Spain rail-collision town/city, Bulgaria first female head of state, World Cup boycott-petition football association, Australian Open bracket questions, TikTok US JV CEO, Deloitte Money League, Oscars nomination markets, NZ landslide location, oldest-paintings island, Greenland deal counterpart and tariff-country markets had no Jan. 16 naming signal strong enough to override existing forecasts.
- The Jan. 16 Greenland tariff threat was broad and did not identify a 15%-to-25% tariff target, so I did not add Denmark to that specific tariff market.

## 2026-01-17 Update

Resolution events:
- Alcaraz's AO second-round opponent resolved to Yannick Hanfmann, matching the draw-based first choice.
- Bangladesh election-party reconsideration resolved to National Citizen Party. I had NCP live but behind BNP/Jamaat. Lesson: when Bangladesh election coverage involves candidate eligibility, citizenship, or Gen-Z/new-party procedural fights, keep NCP high.
- Pakistan's boycott-support country resolved to Bangladesh, matching the high-confidence forecast.
- Portugal runoff opponent resolved to Antonio Jose Seguro, matching the late-poll shift.
- Southern Spain rail-collision markets resolved to Adamuz and Huelva. I missed both. Lesson: when Spanish rail questions are open, wait for route/town reporting where possible and avoid over-weighting the largest nearby Andalusian cities.
- AFCON missed penalty resolved to Brahim Diaz, matching the Morocco attacking/penalty-taker prior.

Updates made:
- Syria/SDF: Jan. 17 Al Jazeera/Reuters-style coverage said the Syrian army took control of Deir Hafer, Maskana and dozens of east Aleppo towns after SDF withdrawal; Deir Hafer was specifically described as under full control. Moved the complete-control city forecast strongly to Deir Hafer, kept Maskana second, and reduced the Raqqa hidden-signal hedge. Nudged Syrian army higher for al-Shaddadi control but kept SDF/US tails because no article directly named al-Shaddadi.
- Ukraine/Russia/US talks: Ukrainian negotiators arrived in the US for Witkoff/Kushner/Driscoll talks, while reporting said documents could be signed at Davos and Russia still needed consultation. Shifted Jan. 23-24 host-city probability toward Davos and Miami, kept Moscow alive, raised US/Switzerland as mediator-country outcomes, and moved the Feb. 4 second-round venue toward Miami/Davos with Moscow still a major hedge.
- Gaza Board of Peace: the founding/expanded list named Rubio, Witkoff, Blair, Kushner, Marc Rowan, Ajay Banga, Robert Gabriel and Nickolay Mladenov, with Turkey/Egypt/Canada leaders invited or accepting. No South Asian government was announced, so I lowered Bangladesh/India/Pakistan for the only South Asian Board-member market while keeping them as the main plausible outcomes.
- Bangladesh T20 World Cup: BCB/ICC reporting now frames Bangladesh's dispute as a relocation request to Sri Lanka rather than an imminent replacement. Lowered UAE/Oman/Nepal probabilities for the team replacing Bangladesh.
- Greenland/tariffs: Trump threatened tariffs on Denmark and several European allies until a Greenland purchase deal is reached, with reporting describing a rise to 25 percent. Added Denmark as the leading tariff-country outcome and shifted the Greenland deal-counterpart market toward Danish officials, especially Lars Lokke Rasmussen and Mette Frederiksen, while keeping Vivian Motzfeldt live.
- Australian Open: Raducanu had difficult scheduling and lingering foot concerns before opening against Sawangkaew, so I trimmed her as Sabalenka's likely third-round opponent and raised Sawangkaew. Djokovic said he lacked "juice in the legs"; I trimmed Djokovic for the men's title and moved Alcaraz/Sinner level. Andreeva's Adelaide title made Emerson Jones's path through that section less likely, so I reduced Jones for the first-main-draw R4/Sabalenka market.
- Oscars: Sentimental Value swept the European Film Awards, so I modestly raised Sentimental Value/Joachim Trier in the nomination-count markets while keeping One Battle After Another, Hamnet and PTA ahead.

Watched but no update:
- Bulgaria first female head of state, World Cup boycott-petition association, Sinner's AO second-round opponent, Zeynep Sonmez AO third-round opponent, Osaka AO third-round opponent, oldest-paintings island, northern New Zealand landslide location, Deloitte Football Money League, TikTok US JV CEO, Gaza child evacuation and Gaza troop-provider markets had no Jan. 17 naming signal strong enough to move.

## 2026-01-18 Update

Resolution events:
- Bulgaria first female head of state resolved to Iliana Iotova, matching the main forecast.
- Al-Shaddadi base control resolved to Syrian army, matching the raised Jan. 17 forecast.
- Syrian army complete-control city resolved to al-Shaddadi, not Deir Hafer. Lesson: when adjacent Syria questions include a named base/location, include that exact location as an outcome even if the title says city and front-line articles emphasize other towns.

Updates made:
- Australian Open women: Raducanu beat Sawangkaew and Potapova beat Lamens, so Sabalenka's third-round opponent market is now almost entirely Raducanu vs Potapova. Sonmez upset Alexandrova, Jacquemot upset Kostyuk, and Putintseva beat Haddad Maia, so the Sonmez third-round-opponent and Sabalenka QF markets were moved away from eliminated seeds toward Putintseva/Jacquemot and Paolini/Svitolina/Tauson/Navarro. For the debutante fourth-round market, removed the Sakatsume exposure and added Antonia Ruzic while keeping Mboko/Jones live pending their first-round result.
- Australian Open men: Alcaraz beat Adam Walton in straight sets and looked solid after rust in the second set; Zverev won but started poorly against Diallo. Nudged the men's title market slightly toward Alcaraz and away from Zverev.
- NFL playoffs: Seattle routed San Francisco 41-6 and advanced to host the NFC championship; Sam Darnold played through an oblique issue. Removed meaningful Brock Purdy exposure, raised Darnold for the NFC Super Bowl QB, raised Seattle for the Super Bowl winner, and trimmed Denver because Bo Nix is out for the playoffs despite the Broncos advancing.
- Ukraine/Russia/US talks: Umerov said two days of Florida talks with Witkoff/Kushner focused on security guarantees and recovery, and that work would continue at the team level in Davos during WEF. Raised Davos sharply for Jan. 23-24 talks and for the Feb. 4 follow-on venue, while keeping Moscow alive because Russia still needs to be included. Raised Switzerland as a host/mediator hedge but kept the US first as the broker.
- Greenland/tariffs: Reuters-style coverage said Trump threatened Denmark, Norway, Sweden, France, Germany, the UK, the Netherlands and Finland with tariffs rising to 25 percent until a Greenland purchase deal is reached. Raised Denmark for the tariff-country market and shifted the Greenland-framework counterpart toward Mette Frederiksen/Lars Lokke Rasmussen rather than Greenland-only figures.
- Board of Peace: Jan. 18 coverage again named Egypt, Turkey, Argentina and Canada as invited/accepting leaders and reported a draft charter with a $1bn permanent-membership buy-in. No South Asian government was named, so I lowered Bangladesh/Pakistan and put India narrowly first on capacity/strategic-fit grounds.
- Bangladesh T20 World Cup: Cricket Ireland said it had definitive assurances it would not move from its Sri Lanka group after reports that BCB asked the ICC about swapping groups. This keeps Bangladesh's participation dispute live and raises replacement risk back somewhat, with UAE still first and Ireland added as a small explicit group-swap tail.
- IAEA/Ukraine: Jan. 18 Ukraine/Russia coverage included Russian blame over Zaporizhzhia-region power cuts and Ukrainian intelligence warnings of planned Russian attacks to disconnect Ukraine from nuclear plants. Raised both Russia and Ukraine for the special nuclear-watchdog meeting request market.

Watched but no update:
- Sinner's AO second-round opponent, Alcaraz's R4 opponent, Naomi Osaka's AO third-round opponent, Madison Keys eliminator, Deloitte Money League, TikTok US JV CEO, World Cup boycott-petition association, oldest-paintings island, northern New Zealand landslide, Gaza child evacuation, Gaza troop-provider, Crans-Montana bail protest, UFC Hall of Fame, wildfire recovery-funds agency, Milan Olympics ICE/DHS protest, Tyson Fury comeback opponent, Pat Cummins replacement and Bisan Owda platform-ban markets had no Jan. 18 naming signal strong enough to move.

## 2026-01-19 Update

Resolution events:
- World Cup boycott-petition football association resolved to KNVB. I missed by not including the Dutch association. Lesson: for boycott-petition markets around Israel/World Cup participation, include KNVB/Netherlands alongside Danish and Scottish associations.
- Sinner's AO second-round opponent resolved to James Duckworth, matching the draw-based first choice.

Updates made:
- Ukraine/Russia/US talks: Moscow Times/Reuters/Axios-style reporting said Kremlin envoy Kirill Dmitriev is expected to meet Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner at Davos, while Ukraine's Rustem Umerov said his team would continue US talks in Davos. Raised Davos heavily for the Jan. 23-24 three-way-talks city and kept the US first for mediator country, with Switzerland a strong host-country hedge. For the later Feb. 4 venue, moved Geneva up because WEF/Davos explains the January venue better than an early-February repeat.
- Board of Peace: Russia and India were explicitly reported among invited countries, with Pakistan also listed among invited governments and only Hungary clearly signed up. Raised India first and Pakistan second for the South Asian Board-member market; lowered Bangladesh because it was not named in the invitation list.
- Greenland/tariffs: Jan. 19 coverage repeated the Denmark-led tariff threat and EU retaliation planning. Raised Denmark slightly further for the 15-to-25 percent tariff-country market, but did not materially move the Greenland-framework counterpart distribution.
- NFL playoffs: the Rams beat the Bears in overtime and the Patriots beat the Texans; final four are Seahawks, Rams, Patriots and Broncos, with Denver forced to start Jarrett Stidham after Bo Nix's injury. Updated the NFC QB market to Darnold vs Stafford and the Super Bowl market to Seahawks/Patriots/Rams/Broncos.
- Australian Open: Victoria Mboko beat Emerson Jones, so moved the debutante fourth-round/Sabalenka market toward Mboko and removed most Jones exposure. Tommy Paul, Alejandro Davidovich Fokina and Reilly Opelka won first-round matches, so moved Alcaraz's likely R4 opponent to Paul/ADF/Opelka and removed Kovacevic. Emma Navarro was eliminated by Magda Linette; Paolini, Svitolina, Tauson and Pegula advanced. Updated Sabalenka QF and Keys-eliminator markets accordingly. Djokovic won his opener in straight sets and looked fit, so restored a modest men's-title probability for him.

Watched but no update:
- Oldest-paintings island, northern New Zealand landslide, Deloitte Money League, TikTok US JV CEO, Gaza child evacuation, Gaza troop-provider, Crans-Montana bail protest, UFC Hall of Fame, wildfire recovery-funds agency, Milan Olympics ICE/DHS protest, Tyson Fury comeback opponent, Bisan Owda platform ban, Netanyahu visitor, West African party dissolution, Epstein/Commerce Secretary, Pat Cummins replacement, Dnipropetrovsk miners bus attack, Balochistan secured town and Greek factory explosion markets had no Jan. 19 naming signal strong enough to move.
